---
title: "Exercise 11.2 — Runge's Phenomenon: AI Comparison and Fact-Checking"
subtitle: "Numerical Analysis 2026 — Lecture 11"
author: "Global Center for Science and Engineering, Waseda University"
format:
  html:
    toc: true
    toc-depth: 3
    code-tools: true
    highlight-style: github
execute:
  echo: true
  warning: false
jupyter: python3
---

## Problem

::: {.callout-tip}
## AI Recommended

AI use is **strongly recommended** for this exercise.  You will consult
two different AI models, compare their responses, and fact-check them
against academic sources.
:::

**Runge's phenomenon** is a classical result in numerical analysis
demonstrating that high-degree polynomial interpolation with equally
spaced nodes can diverge near the endpoints of an interval.

**Your tasks:**

1. Ask **two different** generative AI models (e.g., ChatGPT, Gemini,
   Claude, Copilot, …) the **same** prompt about Runge's phenomenon.
2. Compare the responses in a structured way.
3. Verify the accuracy of both responses using academic sources.
4. Write your own synthesis and reflection.

---

## Part 1 — AI Model Consultation

Use the prompt below (or write your own, but use the **same** prompt for
both models and include it here).

**Suggested prompt:**

> Please explain Runge's phenomenon in numerical analysis.  Include:
> (1) the mathematical definition and context,
> (2) why it occurs,
> (3) a specific example — preferably Runge's original function
>     $f(x) = \frac{1}{1 + 25x^2}$ on $[-1, 1]$,
> (4) methods to mitigate this problem, and
> (5) its practical implications in numerical computing.

### 1.1 Actual Prompt Used

*[Paste the exact prompt you used here — required if different from above.]*

### 1.2 AI Model 1 Response

**AI Tool:** [Name and version, e.g., ChatGPT (GPT-4o), 2026-xx-xx]

**Response:**

*[Paste or summarise the complete response from Model 1 here.]*

### 1.3 AI Model 2 Response

**AI Tool:** [Name and version, e.g., Gemini 2.0 Flash, 2026-xx-xx]

**Response:**

*[Paste or summarise the complete response from Model 2 here.]*

---

## Part 2 — Fact-Checking with Academic Sources

List at least **two** credible references (textbook or journal article)
you used to verify the AI responses.

**References:**

1. *[Author, Year. Title. Publisher / Journal.]*
2. *[Author, Year. Title. Publisher / Journal.]*

**Citation format examples:**

- *Book:* Trefethen, L. N. (2019). *Approximation Theory and
  Approximation Practice* (Extended ed.). SIAM.
- *Paper:* Platte, R. B., Trefethen, L. N., & Kuijlaars, A. B. J. (2011).
  Impossibility of fast stable approximation of analytic functions from
  equispaced samples. *SIAM Review*, 53(2), 308–318.
- *Book:* Burden, R. L., & Faires, J. D. (2011). *Numerical Analysis*
  (9th ed.). Brooks/Cole.
- *Book:* Süli, E., & Mayers, D. (2003). *An Introduction to Numerical
  Analysis*. Cambridge University Press.

::: {.callout-important}
## Choosing credible academic sources

Your references should be **journal articles** or **published books** —
not Wikipedia, blogs, or course notes on their own.  Standard numerical
analysis textbooks and peer-reviewed papers (e.g., in *SIAM Review*) are
appropriate.

**Finding sources on campus:** [Springer Nature Link](https://link.springer.com/)
provides access to many books and papers while you are on campus.  Try
searching for "Numerical Analysis" to locate standard textbooks and
related articles.

**AI-provided citations:** Modern AI models often list sources in their
responses.  Treat these as **starting points only** — confirm that each
citation is real, that the authors and title match, and that the quoted
content is accurate before you rely on it.
:::

**Fact-checking findings:**

*[For each key claim in the AI responses, note whether it was confirmed,
partially wrong, or missing according to your references.]*

---

## Part 3 — Comparative Analysis

| Aspect | AI Model 1 | AI Model 2 | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Mathematical definition | | | |
| Explanation of cause | | | |
| Quality of example | | | |
| Remedies presented | | | |
| Practical implications | | | |
| Errors / omissions | | | |

**Detailed comparison:**

*[Provide a narrative analysis of the similarities and key differences
between the two responses.]*

---

## Part 4 — Synthesis and Reflection

### What the AI models got right

*[Summarise accurate information found in both responses.]*

### What the AI models missed or got wrong

*[Highlight errors, misconceptions, or important omissions.]*

### Lessons learned about AI reliability

*[Reflect on the reliability of AI for mathematical explanations.  When
should you trust AI output and when should you verify it?]*

---

## AI Usage

**Note:** If you used AI, change `[ ]` to `[x]` for the applicable items.

- [ ] I did **not** use AI assistance for this exercise.
- [ ] I used AI assistance for this exercise.

If you used AI, please specify:

**AI Tool(s) Used:**

```
[Name of AI tool(s)]
```

**Prompt(s) Used:**

```
[Copy the prompt(s) here — the prompts from Part 1 count here too]
```

**How AI was used:**

```
[Consultation for Parts 1–4, etc.]
```
